In recent years, zero-shot translation in NMT has attracted widespread attention in academic research  Existing methods are mainly divided into four categories: pivot-based method, transfer learning, multilingual NMT, and unsupervised NMT Pivot-based Method is a common strategy to obtain a source$\rightarrow $target model by introducing a pivot language  This approach is further divided into pivoting and pivot-synthetic

In this paper, we propose a cross-lingual pretraining based transfer approach for the challenging zero-shot translation task, in which source and target languages have no parallel data, while they both have parallel data with a high resource pivot language

During model training, given source$\leftrightarrow $pivot sentence pair, BRLM-HA randomly masks some words in source sentence and leverages alignment information to obtain the aligned words in pivot sentence for masked words  Based on the processed input, BRLM-HA adopts the Transformer BIBREF1 encoder to gain the hidden states for source and pivot sentences respectively

For the masking strategy during training, following BIBREF29, $15\%$ of BPE tokens are selected to be masked  Among the selected tokens, $80\%$ of them are replaced with [MASK] token, $10\%$ are replaced with a random BPE token, and $10\%$ unchanged  The prediction accuracy of masked words is used as a stopping criterion in the pre-training stage

For the more complicated scenario that either the source side or the target side has multiple languages, the encoder and the decoder are also shared across each side languages for efficient deployment of translation between multiple languages Experiments ::: Setup We evaluate our cross-lingual pre-training based transfer approach against several strong baselines on two public datatsets, Europarl BIBREF31 and MultiUN BIBREF32, which contain multi-parallel evaluation data to assess the zero-shot performance

With the aim of building the language invariant representation between source and pivot languages for smooth transfer of the parent model of pivot$\rightarrow $target direction to the child model of source$\rightarrow $target direction, we introduce one monolingual pretraining method and two bilingual pretraining methods to construct an universal encoder for the source and pivot languages

We consider the typical zero-shot translation scenario in which a high resource pivot language has parallel data with both source and target languages, while source and target languages has no parallel data between themselves  Our proposed cross-lingual pretraining based transfer approach for source$\rightarrow $target zero-shot translation is mainly divided into two phrases: the pretraining phase and the transfer phase

The study employs 80K BPE splits for vocabulary and tokenizes sentences using a Perl script, with all data lowercased for the MultiUN corpus. Baselines include traditional transfer learning, pivot-based methods, and multilingual NMT. For fair comparison, a Transformer-big model with 1024 embedding/hidden units, 4096 feed-forward filter size, 6 layers, and 8 heads per layer is used, with a batch size of 2400 and a sentence length limit of 100 BPE tokens. Experiments on public datasets demonstrate significant outperformance of the proposed approaches over strong baseline systems, showing language invariance in both sentence and word level neural representations. Two existing cross-lingual pre-training methods, Masked Language Modeling (MLM) and Translation Language Modeling (TLM), have shown their effectiveness on XNLI cross-lingual classification task BIBREF11, BIBREF28, but these methods have not been well studied on cross-lingual generation tasks in zero-shot condition  We attempt to take advantage of the cross-lingual ability of the two methods for zero-shot translation

Instead of using external aligner tool, BRLM-SA introduces an additional attention layer to learn the alignment information together with model training  In this way, BRLM-SA avoids the effect caused by external wrong alignment information and enables many-to-one soft alignment during model training  Similar with BRLM-HA, the training objective of BRLM-SA is to predict the masked words by not only the surrounding words in source sentence but also the outputs of attention layer

Transfer learning in neural machine translation (NMT) faces challenges due to differing feature distributions between source and pivot languages, leading to inconsistent translations. For instance, semantically similar sentence pairs may not overlap in source space, causing distinct translations in target space. During the transfer phase, a pivot→target NMT model is trained using a cross-lingually pre-trained encoder, with the final model used for source→target translation. To prevent degeneracy, several encoder layers are frozen during training. Updating all parameters can lead to catastrophic forgetting, while freezing all parameters may not be sufficient for the translation task. Freezing the first four layers of the transformer yields the best performance, balancing pre-training and fine-tuning.

Transfer learning in Neural Machine Translation (NMT) avoids pivoting by using a high-resource pivot→target model to initialize a low-resource source→target model, which is then fine-tuned with limited parallel data. Despite some success, this approach struggles in extremely low-resource or zero-resource scenarios due to a language space mismatch or domain shift problem. Transfer learning can be seen as a multi-domain issue where each source language represents a new domain. Fine-tuning helps minimize feature distribution discrepancies but requires parallel data, hindering zero-resource settings. Pivot-based methods, while effective, suffer from computational and parameter inefficiencies and error propagation. Multilingual NMT (MNMT) trains a single model for multiple language pairs, benefiting from transfer learning and simpler deployment. Freezing parameters is a strategy to prevent catastrophic forgetting during transfer learning, as shown in Table TABREF43, which evaluates performance on the MultiUN test set with different layer freezing strategies.

The study investigates Neural Machine Translation (NMT) techniques, focusing on transfer learning, pivot-based methods, and multilingual NMT, using a Transformer-big model. It employs an 80K BPE vocabulary and lowercases sentences for the MultiUN corpus. The research demonstrates significant improvements over baselines, showing language invariance in neural representations. Transfer learning initializes low-resource models with high-resource ones but struggles in zero-resource scenarios due to domain shifts. Pivot-based methods are computationally inefficient, while multilingual NMT faces hyperparameter sensitivity. The study introduces a single encoder-decoder model for efficient translation, outperforming existing methods, especially in zero-shot translation. It uses a Transformer-big model with 1024 embedding/hidden units, 4096 feed-forward size, 6 layers, and 8 heads, with a batch size of 2400 and a 100 BPE token limit. Free

Neural Machine Translation (NMT) excels with large parallel datasets but struggles with low-resource languages, often using pivot languages like English to bridge gaps. Zero-shot translation methods include pivot-based, transfer learning, multilingual NMT, and unsupervised NMT. Multilingual NMT can fail in zero-shot scenarios and is sensitive to hyperparameters. Unsupervised NMT, using monolingual data, shows promise but performs poorly between distant languages. Recent improvements in UNMT involve denoising auto-encoders, SMT, and unsupervised pre-training. Our study introduces approaches that outperform pivoting and multilingual NMT, using a single encoder-decoder model for efficient translation. These methods surpass existing baselines across languages and datasets, especially in zero-shot translation. The research was supported by Alibaba Group and PAPD of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions.

We propose a new transfer learning approach for NMT which uses the cross-lingual language model pre-training to enable a high performance on zero-shot translation We propose a novel pre-training method called BRLM, which can effectively alleviates the distance between different source language spaces Our proposed approach significantly improves zero-shot translation performance, consistently surpassing pivoting and multilingual approaches  Meanwhile, the performance on supervised translation direction remains the same level or even better when using our method Related Work

MLM, a method inspired by BERT's Cloze objective, predicts masked words in monolingual corpora to find shared features across languages, aligning sentence representations. TLM extends MLM by using parallel data; it concatenates sentence pairs, masking words in both, encouraging alignment of source and target language representations. However, TLM resets target sentence positions and lacks explicit alignment.

BRLM, proposed to enhance word-level alignment, uses shared encoders for both sentences, with Hard Alignment (BRLM-HA) and Soft Alignment (BRLM-SA) designs. BRLM aims to infer masked words across languages using context from aligned words, improving alignment and mitigating domain shift issues.

The document describes a pretraining phase for multilingual models, starting with Masked Language Model (MLM) on monolingual data for source and pivot languages, followed by further optimization of Target Language Model (TLM) and Bidirectional Representation Learning Model (BRLM) using bilingual data. Experiments compare MLM+TLM and MLM+BRLM against pivoting, multilingual NMT, and non-pretrained cross-lingual transfer methods, showing significant improvements. MLM alone performs well, but combining it with TLM or BRLM enhances performance, with MLM+BRLM-SA outperforming others. Evaluations of representational invariance across languages confirm that MLM+BRLM-SA achieves better word-level alignment, reducing domain shift issues.

Experiments ::: Analysis ::: The Effect of Freezing Parameters To freeze parameters is a common strategy to avoid catastrophic forgetting in transfer learning BIBREF27  Table TABREF43 shows the performance of transfer learning with freezing different layers on MultiUN test set, in which En$\rightarrow $Ru denotes the parent model, Ar$\rightarrow $Ru and Es$\rightarrow $Ru are two child models, and all models are based on MLM+BRLM-SA

BRLM-HA and BRLM-SA are two methods for training models using source-pivot sentence pairs. In BRLM-HA, words in the source sentence are randomly masked, and alignment information is used to find corresponding words in the pivot sentence. The Transformer encoder processes these sentences to generate hidden states, which are used to predict the masked words, considering both surrounding words and aligned pivot sentence words. Training is symmetric, with roles reversed for BRLM-SA, which also uses an additional attention layer to learn alignments internally, avoiding external alignment errors and enabling many-to-one alignments. For masking, 15% of BPE tokens are selected, with 80% replaced by [MASK], 10% by random tokens, and 10% unchanged. Prediction accuracy of masked words determines the pre-training stage's end.

The study explores zero-shot translation methods for low-resource languages, focusing on cross-lingual transfer without shared vocabularies. It uses Facebook's XLM for initializing the encoder and employs Adam optimizer with specific learning rates. The best approach, MLM+BRLM-SA, significantly outperforms baselines like strong pivoting by 0.9-4.8 BLEU points, especially in translating from Spanish to Arabic and Russian. Back-translation is added to generate pseudo-parallel sentences, improving performance further. MLM+BRLM-SA with back-translation surpasses pivoting by 2.4 BLEU points and MNMT by 4.6 BLEU points on average. Analysis shows that MLM+BRLM-SA has the most stable cross-lingual sentence representations, indicating better transfer learning.

A new pre-training method, BRidge Language Modeling (BRLM), is introduced to leverage source$\leftrightarrow$pivot bilingual data for creating a universal encoder applicable across different languages. This encoder enables direct training of a pivot$\rightarrow$target model, which can then be used for source$\rightarrow$target translation without additional training. The method targets zero-shot translation scenarios with abundant source$\leftrightarrow$pivot and pivot$\leftrightarrow$target data but no direct source$\leftrightarrow$target parallel data. The training process involves pre-training a universal encoder using monolingual or bilingual data, followed by training a pivot$\rightarrow$target model on the pre-trained encoder. To prevent degeneracy, some layers of the encoder are frozen. The challenge lies in ensuring the encoder's representations are language-invariant, facilitating smooth transfer from pivot$\rightarrow$target to source$\rightarrow$target translation. This is achieved through one monolingual and two bilingual pre-training methods designed to

The text discusses extending Masked Language Model (MLM) to a bilingual setting through Bilingual Retrieval Language Model (BRLM). BRLM uses either an external aligner tool (BRLM-HA) or an additional attention layer (BRLM-SA) to incorporate alignment information during training. In BRLM-HA, an external aligner tool is used on source-pivot parallel data to extract alignment information. The attention layer in BRLM-SA is a multi-head attention mechanism, where queries are from the masked source sentence, and keys and values are from the pivot sentence. While MLM and Target Language Model (TLM) can learn some implicit alignment, this process is inefficient due to limited shared word pieces. BRLM-HA performs worse than BRLM-SA and TLM on Fr-En, possibly due to incorrect alignment from the external tool. The study also notes a decrease in cosine similarity as the number of

This paper presents BRLM, a novel transfer learning approach for neural machine translation (NMT) that enhances zero-shot translation performance. BRLM ensures a common feature space across different source languages, surpassing traditional pivoting and multilingual methods. The approach consists of pretraining and transfer phases. Pretraining uses Masked Language Modeling (MLM) and Translation Language Modeling (TLM) on monolingual and bilingual data to create a shared feature space. The transfer phase fine-tunes the model for specific translation tasks. Evaluations on Europarl and MultiUN datasets show superior zero-shot translation capabilities.

The study compares MLM+TLM and MLM+BRLM against pivoting, multilingual NMT, and non-pretrained cross-lingual transfer methods, demonstrating significant improvements. MLM+BRLM-SA outperforms others, especially in translating from Spanish to Arabic and Russian, by 0.9-4.8 BLEU points. Back

This paper introduces a novel transfer learning approach for neural machine translation (NMT) that leverages cross-lingual pre-training to enhance zero-shot translation performance. The proposed method, called BRLM, ensures that different source languages share a common feature space, facilitating smoother zero-shot translation. It surpasses traditional pivoting and multilingual approaches while maintaining or improving supervised translation performance.

The approach involves two phases: pretraining and transfer. Pretraining uses Masked Language Modeling (MLM) and Translation Language Modeling (TLM) to create a shared feature space across languages. The transfer phase then fine-tunes the model for specific translation tasks. The method is evaluated on the Europarl and MultiUN datasets, demonstrating superior zero-shot translation capabilities compared to existing baselines. This technique is particularly effective in scenarios where source and target languages share parallel data with a high-resource pivot language but have no direct parallel data between them.

The study employs 80K BPE splits for vocabulary and tokenizes sentences using a Perl script, with all data lowercased for the MultiUN corpus. Experiments on public datasets demonstrate significant outperformance of the proposed approaches over strong baseline systems, showing language invariance in both sentence and word level neural representations. Two existing cross-lingual pre-training methods, Masked Language Modeling (MLM) and Translation Language Modeling (TLM), have shown their effectiveness on XNLI cross-lingual classification task BIBREF11, BIBREF28, but these methods have not been well studied on cross-lingual generation tasks in zero-shot condition  We attempt to take advantage of the cross-lingual ability of the two methods for zero-shot translation

 Instead of using external aligner tool, BRLM-SA introduces an additional attention layer to learn the alignment information together with model training  In this way, BRLM-SA avoids the effect caused by external wrong alignment information and enables many-to-one soft alignment during model training  Similar with BRLM-HA, the training objective of BRLM-SA is to predict the masked words by not only the surrounding words in source sentence but also the outputs of attention layer

Transfer learning in neural machine translation (NMT) faces challenges due to differing feature distributions between source and pivot languages, leading to inconsistent translations. For instance, semantically similar sentence pairs may not overlap in source space, causing distinct translations in target space. During the transfer phase, a pivot→target NMT model is trained using a cross-lingually pre-trained encoder, with the final model used for source→target translation. To prevent degeneracy, several encoder layers are frozen during training. Updating all parameters can lead to catastrophic forgetting, while freezing all parameters may not be sufficient for the translation task. Freezing the first four layers of the transformer yields the best performance, balancing pre-training and fine-tuning.

Transfer learning in Neural Machine Translation (NMT) avoids pivoting by using a high-resource pivot→target model to initialize a low-resource source→target model, which is then fine-tuned with limited parallel data. Despite some success, this approach struggles in extremely low-resource or zero-resource scenarios due to a language space mismatch or domain shift problem. Transfer learning can be seen as a multi-domain issue where each source language represents a new domain. Fine-tuning helps minimize feature distribution discrepancies but requires parallel data, hindering zero-resource settings. Pivot-based methods, while effective, suffer from computational and parameter inefficiencies and error propagation. Multilingual NMT (MNMT) trains a single model for multiple language pairs, benefiting from transfer learning and simpler deployment. Freezing parameters is a strategy to prevent catastrophic forgetting during transfer learning, as shown in Table TABREF43, which evaluates performance on the MultiUN test set with different layer freezing strategies. The study investigates Neural Machine Translation (NMT) techniques, focusing on transfer learning, pivot-based methods, and multilingual NMT, using a Transformer-big model. It employs an 80K BPE vocabulary and lowercases sentences for the MultiUN corpus. The research demonstrates significant improvements over baselines, showing language invariance in neural representations. Transfer learning initializes low-resource models with high-resource ones but struggles in zero-resource scenarios due to domain shifts. Pivot-based methods are computationally inefficient, while multilingual NMT faces hyperparameter sensitivity. The study introduces a single encoder-decoder model for efficient translation, outperforming existing methods, especially in zero-shot translation. It uses a Transformer-big model with 1024 embedding/hidden units, 4096 feed-forward size, 6 layers, and 8 heads, with a batch size of 2400 and a 100 BPE token limit. Free

Neural Machine Translation (NMT) excels with large parallel datasets but struggles with low-resource languages, often using pivot languages like English to bridge gaps. Zero-shot translation methods include pivot-based, transfer learning, multilingual NMT, and unsupervised NMT. Multilingual NMT can fail in zero-shot scenarios and is sensitive to hyperparameters. Unsupervised NMT, using monolingual data, shows promise but performs poorly between distant languages. Recent improvements in UNMT involve denoising auto-encoders, SMT, and unsupervised pre-training. Our study introduces approaches that outperform pivoting and multilingual NMT, using a single encoder-decoder model for efficient translation. These methods surpass existing baselines across languages and datasets, especially in zero-shot translation. The research was supported by Alibaba Group and PAPD of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions.

We propose a new transfer learning approach for NMT which uses the cross-lingual language model pre-training to enable a high performance on zero-shot translation We propose a novel pre-training method called BRLM, which can effectively alleviates the distance between different source language spaces Our proposed approach significantly improves zero-shot translation performance, consistently surpassing pivoting and multilingual approaches  Meanwhile, the performance on supervised translation direction remains the same level or even better when using our method Related Work

MLM, a method inspired by BERT's Cloze objective, predicts masked words in monolingual corpora to find shared features across languages, aligning sentence representations. TLM extends MLM by using parallel data; it concatenates sentence pairs, masking words in both, encouraging alignment of source and target language representations. However, TLM resets target sentence positions and lacks explicit alignment.  BRLM, proposed to enhance word-level alignment, uses shared encoders for both sentences, with Hard Alignment (BRLM-HA) and Soft Alignment (BRLM-SA) designs. BRLM aims to infer masked words across languages using context from aligned words, improving alignment and mitigating domain shift issues.

The document describes a pretraining phase for multilingual models, starting with Masked Language Model (MLM) on monolingual data for source and pivot languages, followed by further optimization of Target Language Model (TLM) and Bidirectional Representation Learning Model (BRLM) using bilingual data. Experiments compare MLM+TLM and MLM+BRLM against pivoting, multilingual NMT, and non-pretrained cross-lingual transfer methods, showing significant improvements. MLM alone performs well, but combining it with TLM or BRLM enhances performance, with MLM+BRLM-SA outperforming others. Evaluations of representational invariance across languages confirm that MLM+BRLM-SA achieves better word-level alignment, reducing domain shift issues.

Experiments ::: Analysis ::: The Effect of Freezing Parameters To freeze parameters is a common strategy to avoid catastrophic forgetting in transfer learning BIBREF27  Table TABREF43 shows the performance of transfer learning with freezing different layers on MultiUN test set, in which En$\rightarrow $Ru denotes the parent model, Ar$\rightarrow $Ru and Es$\rightarrow $Ru are two child models, and all models are based on MLM+BRLM-SA

BRLM-HA and BRLM-SA are two methods for training models using source-pivot sentence pairs. In BRLM-HA, words in the source sentence are randomly masked, and alignment information is used to find corresponding words in the pivot sentence. The Transformer encoder processes these sentences to generate hidden states, which are used to predict the masked words, considering both surrounding words and aligned pivot sentence words. Training is symmetric, with roles reversed for BRLM-SA, which also uses an additional attention layer to learn alignments internally, avoiding external alignment errors and enabling many-to-one alignments. For masking, 15% of BPE tokens are selected, with 80% replaced by [MASK], 10% by random tokens, and 10% unchanged. Prediction accuracy of masked words determines the pre-training stage's end.

The study explores zero-shot translation methods for low-resource languages, focusing on cross-lingual transfer without shared vocabularies. It uses Facebook's XLM for initializing the encoder and employs Adam optimizer with specific learning rates. The best approach, MLM+BRLM-SA, significantly outperforms baselines like strong pivoting by 0.9-4.8 BLEU points, especially in translating from Spanish to Arabic and Russian. Back-translation is added to generate pseudo-parallel sentences, improving performance further. MLM+BRLM-SA with back-translation surpasses pivoting by 2.4 BLEU points and MNMT by 4.6 BLEU points on average. Analysis shows that MLM+BRLM-SA has the most stable cross-lingual sentence representations, indicating better transfer learning.

A new pre-training method, BRidge Language Modeling (BRLM), is introduced to leverage source$\leftrightarrow$pivot bilingual data for creating a universal encoder applicable across different languages. This encoder enables direct training of a pivot$\rightarrow$target model, which can then be used for source$\rightarrow$target translation without additional training. The method targets zero-shot translation scenarios with abundant source$\leftrightarrow$pivot and pivot$\leftrightarrow$target data but no direct source$\leftrightarrow$target parallel data. The training process involves pre-training a universal encoder using monolingual or bilingual data, followed by training a pivot$\rightarrow$target model on the pre-trained encoder. To prevent degeneracy, some layers of the encoder are frozen. The challenge lies in ensuring the encoder's representations are language-invariant, facilitating smooth transfer from pivot$\rightarrow$target to source$\rightarrow$target translation. The text discusses extending Masked Language Model (MLM) to a bilingual setting through Bilingual Retrieval Language Model (BRLM). BRLM uses either an external aligner tool (BRLM-HA) or an additional attention layer (BRLM-SA) to incorporate alignment information during training. In BRLM-HA, an external aligner tool is used on source-pivot parallel data to extract alignment information. The attention layer in BRLM-SA is a multi-head attention mechanism, where queries are from the masked source sentence, and keys and values are from the pivot sentence. While MLM and Target Language Model (TLM) can learn some implicit alignment, this process is inefficient due to limited shared word pieces. This paper presents BRLM, a novel transfer learning approach for neural machine translation (NMT) that enhances zero-shot translation performance. BRLM ensures a common feature space across different source languages, surpassing traditional pivoting and multilingual methods. The approach consists of pretraining and transfer phases. Pretraining uses Masked Language Modeling (MLM) and Translation Language Modeling (TLM) on monolingual and bilingual data to create a shared feature space. The transfer phase fine-tunes the model for specific translation tasks. Evaluations on Europarl and MultiUN datasets show superior zero-shot translation capabilities.  The study compares MLM+TLM and MLM+BRLM against pivoting, multilingual NMT, and non-pretrained cross-lingual transfer methods, demonstrating significant improvements. MLM+BRLM-SA outperforms others, especially in translating from Spanish to Arabic and Russian, by 0.9-4.8 BLEU points. Back

This paper introduces a novel transfer learning approach for neural machine translation (NMT) that leverages cross-lingual pre-training to enhance zero-shot translation performance. The proposed method, called BRLM, ensures that different source languages share a common feature space, facilitating smoother zero-shot translation. It surpasses traditional pivoting and multilingual approaches while maintaining or improving supervised translation performance.  The approach involves two phases: pretraining and transfer. Pretraining uses Masked Language Modeling (MLM) and Translation Language Modeling (TLM) to create a shared feature space across languages. The transfer phase then fine-tunes the model for specific translation tasks. The method is evaluated on the Europarl and MultiUN datasets, demonstrating superior zero-shot translation capabilities compared to existing baselines. This technique is particularly effective in scenarios where source and target languages share parallel data with a high-resource pivot language but have no direct parallel data between them.
